perturbation.original_text: At all times from and after the date of this Agreement and until the expiration of the Restricted Period, Seller and Guarantor shall not: (a) directly or indirectly engage in, be employed by, own, manage, operate, provide financing to, control or participate in the ownership, management or control of, or otherwise have an interest (whether, subject to Section 5, as a stockholder, director, officer, employee, representative, subcontractor, partner, consultant, proprietor, agent or otherwise) in, or cause, authorize, aid or assist any other person to own, manage, operate, provide financing to, control or otherwise have an interest in, any business or any person who is engaged in any business that directly or indirectly competes or intends to compete with the Restricted Business anywhere in the world, unless Seller or Guarantor purchase or own less than five percent (5%) of capital stock in a publicly held company; or (b) directly, indirectly or otherwise by letters, circulars or advertisements, and whether for itself or on behalf of any other person, canvass or solicit or, directly or indirectly, cause or authorize to be solicited, or enter into or effect, or, directly or indirectly, cause or authorize to be entered into or effected, any business or orders for businesses competing with the Restricted Business from any person who (i) at the time of the Agreement or within two years prior to the date of the Agreement, has been, a customer or client, or (ii) is an active prospect to be a customer or client, in each case, of the Seller at the time of the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: At all times from and after the date of this Agreement and until the expiration of the Restricted Period, Seller and Guarantor shall endeavor to not: (a) directly or indirectly engage in, be employed by, own, manage, operate, provide financing to, control or participate in the ownership, management or control of, or otherwise have an interest (whether, subject to Section 5, as a stockholder, director, officer, employee, representative, subcontractor, partner, consultant, proprietor, agent or otherwise) in, or cause, authorize, aid or assist any other person to own, manage, operate, provide financing to, control or otherwise have an interest in, any business or any person who is engaged in any business that directly or indirectly competes or intends to compete with the Restricted Business anywhere in the world, unless Seller or Guarantor purchase or own less than five percent (5%) of capital stock in a publicly held company; or (b) directly, indirectly or otherwise by letters, circulars or advertisements, and whether for itself or on behalf of any other person, canvass or solicit or, directly or indirectly, cause or authorize to be solicited, or enter into or effect, or, directly or indirectly, cause or authorize to be entered into or effected, any business or orders for businesses competing with the Restricted Business from any person who (i) at the time of the Agreement or within two years prior to the date of the Agreement, has been, a customer or client, or (ii) is an active prospect to be a customer or client, in each case, of the Seller at the time of the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor to not' weakens the non-compete clause, making it ambiguous whether the Seller and Guarantor have a strict prohibition or merely a weaker obligation to try to avoid competition. This introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law M.G.L. c. 149, § 24L governs non-compete agreements. The modification from 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor to not' introduces ambiguity, which can be seen as an unreasonable restriction. This conflicts with the spirit and intent of M.G.L. c. 149, § 24L, which strives to ensure that non-competes are clearly defined and reasonable to protect employees.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 24L Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 24L Massachusetts Noncompetition Agreement Act Section 24L Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 24L: Massachusetts Noncompetition Agreement Act Section 24L. (a) As used in this section, the following words shall have the following meanings:- ''Business entity'', any person or group of persons performing or engaging in any activity, enterprise, profession or occupation for gain, benefit, advantage or livelihood, whether for profit or not for profit, including but not limited to corporations, limited liability companies, limited partnerships or limited liability partnerships. ''Employee'', an individual who is considered an employee under section 148B of this chapter; provided, however, that the term ''employee'', as used in this section, shall also include independent contractors under section 148B. ''Forfeiture agreement'', an agreement that imposes adverse financial consequences on a former employee as a result of the termination of an employment relationship, regardless of whether the employee engages in competitive activities following cessation of the employment relationship. Forfeiture agreements do not include forfeiture for competition agreements. ''Forfeiture for competition agreement'', an agreement that by its terms or through the manner in which it is enforced imposes adverse financial consequences on a former employee as a result of the termination of an employment relationship if the employee engages in competitive activities. ''Garden leave clause'', a provision within a noncompetition agreement by which an employer agrees to pay the employee during the restricted period, provided that such provision shall become effective upon termination of employment unless the restriction

perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor to not' weakens the enforceability of the non-compete agreement. While the provided snippets define terms related to non-compete agreements under Massachusetts law M.G.L. c. 149, § 24L, they don't explicitly state that ambiguity in non-compete clauses is a violation. However, the Law Explanation states that the modification introduces ambiguity which can be seen as an unreasonable restriction, conflicting with the intent of M.G.L. c. 149, § 24L. This ambiguity directly impacts the obligation of the Seller and Guarantor, weakening a core element of the original agreement, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Seller and Guarantor recognize that all such Confidential Information is and shall remain the sole property of the Buyer, free of any rights of Seller or Guarantor, and acknowledges that the Buyer and its Affiliates have a vested interest in assuring that all such Confidential Information remains secret and confidential. Therefore, the Seller and Guarantor agree that at all times from after the date hereof, they will not, directly or indirectly, without the prior written consent of the Buyer, disclose to any person, firm, company or other entity (other than the Buyer or any of its Affiliates) any Confidential Information, except to the extent that (i) any such Confidential Information becomes generally available to the public or trade, other than as a result of a breach by the Seller or Guarantor of this Section 3, or (ii) any such Confidential Information becomes available to the Seller or Guarantor on a non-confidential basis from a source other than the Seller, Guarantor, Buyer or any of their Affiliates or advisors; provided, that such source is not known by the Seller or Guarantor to be bound by a confidentiality agreement with, or other obligation of secrecy to, the Seller, Guarantor, Buyer or another party.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller and Guarantor recognize that all such Confidential Information is and should remain the sole property of the Buyer, free of any rights of Seller or Guarantor, and acknowledges that the Buyer and its Affiliates may have a vested interest in assuring that all such Confidential Information remains secret and confidential. Therefore, the Seller and Guarantor agree that at all times from after the date hereof, they will try to not, directly or indirectly, without expressed consent of the Buyer, disclose to any person, firm, company or other entity (other than the Buyer or any of its Affiliates) any Confidential Information, except to the extent that (i) any such Confidential Information becomes generally available to the public or trade, other than possibly as a result of a breach by the Seller or Guarantor of this Section 3, or (ii) any such Confidential Information becomes available to the Seller or Guarantor on a non-confidential basis from a source other than the Seller, Guarantor, Buyer or any of their Affiliates or advisors; provided, that said source has no known agreement to the Seller or Guarantor to be bound by a confidentiality agreement with, or other obligation of secrecy to, the Seller, Guarantor, Buyer or another party.
perturbation.explanation: Several changes introduce ambiguity: 'should remain' instead of 'shall remain' weakens the assertion of ownership; 'may have' instead of 'have' makes the Buyer's interest uncertain; 'try to not' instead of 'will not' weakens the prohibition against disclosure; 'expressed consent' instead of 'prior written consent' introduces uncertainty, 'possibly as a result' lowers the certainty of liability and 'said source has no known agreement to the Seller or Guarantor' makes it difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. The series of modifications weakens the confidentiality obligations to the point where the protection of trade secrets and confidential information is uncertain. This could be argued as an unfair or deceptive practice if it undermines the Buyer's reasonable expectation of confidentiality.

perturbation.justification: The original contract ensured confidentiality through strong language ('shall remain,' 'have a vested interest,' 'will not'). The changed contract uses ambiguous terms ('should remain,' 'may have,' 'try to not,' 'expressed consent,' 'possibly as a result,' 'said source has no known agreement'). The Law Explanation states that M.G.L. c. 93A prohibits unfair or deceptive practices, and the changes weaken the confidentiality obligations to a point of uncertainty, potentially undermining the Buyer's reasonable expectation of confidentiality. The Perturbation Explanation also describes how each change weakens a legal obligation to the point of ambiguity and difficult enforcement. The changes introduce ambiguities that collectively weaken the enforceability of confidentiality, contradicting the intent to protect confidential information, thus creating a legal contradiction under the Ambiguities definition. The scraped law snippets do not directly address this, but their description of Chapter 93A suggests a violation because deceptive practices around confidentiality are not allowed.

perturbation.original_text: At all times from and after the date of this Agreement and until the expiration of the Restricted Period, Seller and Guarantor shall not, directly, indirectly or otherwise by letters, circulars or advertisements, and whether for themselves or on behalf of any other person: (a) solicit or, directly or indirectly, cause to be solicited for employment, any persons who (i) are, at the time of solicitation of employment, employees of the Seller, Buyer or any of their respective Affiliates, or (ii) are, at the time of solicitation of employment, sales representatives or employees thereof, retained by the Buyer or any of its Affiliates; or (b) employ or, directly or indirectly, cause to be employed, any persons who (i) are, at the time of such action, employees of the Buyer or any of its Affiliates, or (ii) are, at the time of such action, sales representatives or employees thereof, retained by the Buyer or any of its Affiliates; provided, however, that this Section 4 shall not prohibit Seller or Guarantor from employing or soliciting the employment any person who (A) is an employee of Seller as of the Agreement Date and (B) is not offered employment by Buyer as of the Agreement Date.
perturbation.changed_text: At all times from and after the date of this Agreement and until the expiration of the Restricted Period, Seller and Guarantor should aim to not, directly, indirectly or otherwise by letters, circulars or advertisements, and whether for themselves or on behalf of any other person: (a) solicit or, with reasonable effort, directly or indirectly, cause to be solicited for employment, any persons who (i) are, at the time of solicitation of employment, employees of the Seller, Buyer or any of their respective Affiliates, or (ii) are, at the time of solicitation of employment, sales representatives or employees thereof, retained by the Buyer or any of its Affiliates; or (b) employ or, directly or indirectly, cause to possibly be employed, any persons who (i) are, at the time of such action, employees of the Buyer or any of its Affiliates, or (ii) are, at the time of such action, sales representatives or employees thereof, retained by the Buyer or any of its Affiliates; provided, that this Section 4 shall not absolutely prohibit Seller or Guarantor from employing or soliciting the employment any person who (A) is an employee of Seller as of the Agreement Date and (B) is not offered employment by Buyer as of the Agreement Date.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications 'should aim to not', 'with reasonable effort', 'cause to possibly be employed' and 'shall not absolutely prohibit' all introduce ambiguity and reduce the enforceability of the non-solicitation clause. It weakens the prohibition against soliciting and employing.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law M.G.L. c. 149, § 24L governs non-compete agreements. By weakening the non-solicitation clause and introducing ambiguity, these changes could render the clause unreasonable in scope or application, conflicting with M.G.L. c. 149, § 24L's requirement that non-compete agreements be narrowly tailored and reasonable to protect legitimate business interests without unduly restricting an individual's ability to work.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity in the non-solicitation clause, making it difficult to enforce, as explained in the Perturbation Explanation. The original text had clear prohibitions, whereas the changed text uses terms like 'should aim to not', 'with reasonable effort', 'cause to possibly be employed' and 'shall not absolutely prohibit', weakening the obligations. The Law Explanation states this could render the clause unreasonable, conflicting with the requirement that non-compete agreements be narrowly tailored. Although the snippets do not explicitly state the contradiction, they define 'employee' and refer to the 'Massachusetts Noncompetition Agreement Act', laying the groundwork for the legal framework that the change undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Therefore, Seller and Guarantor acknowledges and agrees that, in addition to any other remedy which the Buyer may have at law or in equity, the Buyer shall be entitled to injunctive relief or other equitable remedies in the event of any such breach or threatened breach. Seller and Guarantor further acknowledges and agrees that monetary damages would be insufficient to compensate the Buyer in the event of a breach by Seller or Guarantor of any of the covenants or provisions contained herein, and that in the event of a breach thereof, the Buyer shall be entitled to specific performance of the obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Therefore, Seller and Guarantor acknowledge and agree that, in addition to any other remedy which the Buyer may have at law or in equity, the Buyer might be entitled to injunctive relief or other equitable remedies in the event of any possible breach or threatened breach. Seller and Guarantor further acknowledges and agrees that monetary damages could possibly be insufficient to compensate the Buyer in the event of a breach by Seller or Guarantor of any of the covenants or provisions contained herein, and that in the event of a breach thereof, the Buyer may be entitled to specific performance of the obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall be entitled' with 'might be entitled', 'possible breach' with 'any possible breach' and 'could possibly be insufficient' with 'would be insufficient' significantly weakens the Buyer's right to injunctive relief and specific performance, making the enforceability of these remedies uncertain. It turns definitive statements into speculative ones.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 93A primarily deals with unfair trade practices, § 11 allows businesses to sue other businesses for unfair competition. By undermining the remedies available to the Buyer (injunctive relief and specific performance), the Seller and Guarantor are potentially engaging in a practice that makes it harder for the Buyer to protect its legitimate business interests, potentially constituting unfair competition if the weakened remedies incentivize breaches of the non-compete/non-disclosure agreement. The changes make the remedy of injunctive relief uncertain, thus, potentially inadequate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 11 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 11 Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 11: Persons engaged in business; actions for unfair trade practices; class actions; damages; injunction; costs Section 11. Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Such person, if he has not suffered any loss of money or property, may obtain such an injunction if it can be shown that the aforementioned unfair method of competition, act or practice may have the effect of causing such loss of money or property. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of competition or the unfair or deceptive act or practice has caused similar injury to numerous other persons similarly situated and if the court finds in a preliminary hearing that he adequately and fairly represents such other persons, bring the action on behalf of himself and such other similarly injured and

Any person who engages in the conduct of any trade or commerce and who suffers any loss of money or property, real or personal, as a result of the use or employment by another person who engages in any trade or commerce of an unfair method of competition or an unfair or deceptive act or practice declared unlawful by section two or by any rule or regulation issued under paragraph (c) of section two may, as hereinafter provided, bring an action in the superior court, or in the housing court as provided in section three of chapter one hundred and eighty-five C, whether by way of original complaint, counterclaim, cross-claim or third-party action for damages and such equitable relief, including an injunction, as the court deems to be necessary and proper. Any persons entitled to bring such action may, if the use or employment of the unfair method of comp
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the buyer's remedies of injunctive relief and specific performance by changing "shall be entitled" to "might be entitled" and introducing uncertainty with phrases like "possible breach" and "could possibly be insufficient." This undermines the certainty of recourse for the Buyer, potentially leading to unfair competition as described in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippets 1 and 2, derived from Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A, Section 11, allow a business to bring an action for damages and equitable relief, including an injunction, against another business engaging in unfair methods of competition. By making the availability of injunctive relief and specific performance uncertain, the changed contract could hinder the Buyer's ability to protect its business interests, contradicting the intent of Chapter 93A, Section 11. The original text clearly stipulated the buyer's right to injunctive relief and specific performance, whereas the modified text introduces ambiguity which undermines that right.
